title: We need nuclear weapons to stop nuclear war.
podcast_name: Saving the World From Bad Ideas
summary: рҹ”Қ Episode Summary: In this urgent and wide-ranging conversation, Mark Lynas is joined by leading climate scientist and nuclear winter expert Alan Robock to confront one of the most dangerous myths of our time: that nuclear weapons keep us safe. Alan lays out why deterrence is a flawed and suicidal strategy, how even a "limited" nuclear war would trigger global famine and societal collapse, and why the existence of nuclear weapons means their eventual use is a matter of when, not if. They also discuss the atmospheric science of nuclear winter, parallels to the asteroid that killed the dinosaurs, the threats posed by solar geoengineering, and why total nuclear abolition is not only possible вҖ” but urgently necessary. This is a masterclass in existential risk вҖ” and why we ignore it at our peril. рҹ§  Topics Discussed: рҹҡ« Why nuclear deterrence is a myth вҖ” and how luck has saved us so far вҳ пёҸ Nuclear winter: how cities burning would darken and freeze the planet рҹҢҫ Nuclear famine: why over a billion people could starve even after a "small" war рҹ”Ҙ From Hiroshima to today: how firestorms drive catastrophic global cooling рҹҢҚ The Southern HemisphereвҖҷs relative survival вҖ” and why itвҖҷs not so simple вңҠ The Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons and ICANвҖҷs Nobel Peace Prize рҹӣ°пёҸ How volcanic eruptions and wildfire smoke prove the nuclear winter theory рҹҰ– What the dinosaurs can teach us about the end of the world рҹӣ‘ Geoengineering: why "climate intervention" may be as dangerous as the problem рҹ’¬ Why humanity must choose: the end of nuclear weapons or the end of us рҹ‘Ҫ The Drake equation, Fermi paradox вҖ” and why advanced civilizations may self-destruct рҹ‘ЁвҖҚрҹҸ« Guest Bio: Alan Robock is a Distinguished Professor of Environmental Sciences at Rutgers University. He is one of the worldвҖҷs leading authorities on nuclear winter, climate modeling, and the atmospheric consequences of both nuclear war and geoengineering. Alan is a veteran campaigner for nuclear disarmament and an award-winning researcher committed to educating the world about the existential threats we face. рҹ“ҡ Recommended Reading & Resources: Earth in Flames: Nuclear Winter and How to Prevent It вҖ“ Alan Robock & Brian Toon (out June 2025) Nature Food (2022) вҖ“ Global famine after nuclear war paper ICAN вҖ“ International Campaign to Abolish Nuclear Weapons Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons (UN) The Open Philanthropy Project Future of Life Institute рҹ’¬ Quote Highlights: вҖңDeterrence only works if youвҖҷre willing to commit suicide. ThatвҖҷs not a strategy вҖ” thatвҖҷs madness.вҖқ вҖ” Alan Robock вҖңNuclear winter is not a theory from the 1980s. ItвҖҷs physics. Block the sun, and the planet freezes.вҖқ вҖ” Alan Robock вҖңEven a limited nuclear war could kill two billion people by famine alone.вҖқ вҖ” Alan Robock вҖңYou can dismantle nuclear weapons. We had 70,000 once. Now we have 12,000. We can go to zero.вҖқ вҖ” Alan Robock вҖңThe existence of nuclear weapons guarantees their eventual use вҖ” unless we abolish them.вҖқ вҖ” Alan Robock

title: 200 -  FaГ§ade flammability across scales and standards with Guillermo Rein and Matt Bonner
summary: Episode 200! And for this special episode,В  I've travelled to London to interview Prof. Guillermo Rein and Dr Matt Bonner on a piece of research carried out at Imperial College London, with the experiments performed in our laboratory at the ITB. In this episode, we discuss the concept of flammability of the building facades and how this flammability is assessed with different testing methods available in the world. You could argue that every country has their own method, and in some cases, they use those methods even with varying criteria of acceptance. Even though the methods are as different as they can be, they all claim they test for fire safety of the external faГ§ade and are used as the basis for local regulatory regimes. Knowing that so many methods exist, we approached this with a question: Will they agree on ranking different facades? Will they show us the same results, or will each show us something else? And this question is inspired by Prof. Howard Emmons, who in 1968 went into a similar endeavour with building materials. Back then, Emmons said: вҖңSuch profound disagreement between serious attempts to measure combustibility points out better than any argument that we really donвҖҷt know what we are talking about when we say, вҖҳthis is more combustible than thatвҖҷ; вҖҳthis is a more safe building material than thatвҖҷвҖқ. In this podcast episode, we discuss a series of 25 experiments: testing five facades, two ETICS and three rainscreen facades with a varying degree of use of combustible materials. All the material combinations were built by us in the same way, and then assessed using five test standards:В  The Polish method PN-B-02867,В The international screening method ISO 13785-1 (smaller corner configuration),В The German method DIN 4102-20,В The American method NFPA 285, also used globallyand the British BS 8414, also highly influential over the world and the basis for the new harmonised EU approach.We go into the background and rationale of the experiments, an overview of the testing methods as well as into qualitative and quantitative findings of the study. Once the paper is published, I will update the shownotes with a link here :) For now, you may also want to revisit previous episodes of Fire Science Show discussing the fire safety of facades вҖ“В  It all started with episode 4 with Matt Bonner: https://www.firescienceshow.com/004-facade-fires-and-ai-with-matt-bonner/An overview of current Issues with Eleni Asimakopoulou: https://www.firescienceshow.com/124-advancements-in-fire-safety-of-facades-with-eleni-asimakopoulou/And some interesting facts about SBI method with Rudolf van Mierlo (and their development of faГ§ade testing method): https://www.firescienceshow.com/140-development-and-implementation-of-the-sbi-test-with-rudolf-van-mierlo/This research was funded by The Berkeley Group. The experimental part was performed at the Building Research Institute ITB, with a group of tests with the Polish method performed as part of our statutory research NZP-130. Thank you for being with the Fire Science Show for 200 episodes! Huge shoutout to the OFR for enabling this project and allowing me to share insights like this with all of you in an open-access repository!!! ---- The Fire Science Show is produced by the Fire Science Media in collaboration with OFR Consultants. title: Is our understanding of light completely wrong? Two consciousness theories go head-to-head; decoding dolphin whistles
podcast_name: The world, the universe and us
summary: Episode 301 Our understanding of the nature of light might be completely wrong. The double-slit experiment is one of the most famous experiments in physics and is how weвҖҷve understood light for over 200 years. But a team is now suggesting weвҖҷve got the interpretation all wrong - that light is in fact not a wave and is only made up of particles. If true, this is major. The authors of this study were left scrambling as вҖңall hell broke looseвҖқ when they made the discovery. And the best part? Dark photons may be involved. A 7 year study into the nature of consciousness has reached its conclusion - pitting two major theories against one another. Integrated information theory suggests levels of consciousness exist in any system, including rocks and fetuses. Global neuronal workspace theory claims specific brain regions are responsible for consciousness. After 256 brain scans - are we any closer to an answer? A decades-long study involving 170 wild dolphins has allowed biologists to decode their whistles. Hear dolphins recorded in the waters of Sarasota, Florida, which show how the animals communicate in various ways - and even give themselves names.
